extracted_rules.resource: Protection from medical complications in illness
verbatim: Though many women fell ill, they were fewer than the men and less frequently died. But the great majority had difficult childbirth, and after giving birth they would fall ill, and these especially died, as did the daughter of Telebulus on the sixth day after delivery. [...] all to my knowledge had abortions if they chanced to fall ill when with child.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author identifies a specific heightened vulnerability and negative medical outcome (death/abortion) for women linked to the physiological states of childbirth and pregnancy during the epidemic.

verbatim: From all the diseases some died, but the greatest number from these fevers, especially children—those just weaned, older children of eight or ten years, and those approaching puberty.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that the mortality rate of the described fevers was highest among the specific age group of children and adolescents.

extracted_rules.resource: Preservation of sensory and motor faculties
verbatim: Older people, and those whose natural heat is failing, have paralysis or raving or blindness.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes a rule that advanced age determines the specific clinical manifestation of the disease as loss of motor control or sight, compared to younger victims.

verbatim: Many had swellings beside one ear, or both ears [...] The sufferers were youths, young men, and men in their prime, usually those who frequented the wrestling school and gymnasia.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies individuals associated with specific occupational/lifestyle spaces (the gymnasium/wrestling school) as the primary group losing their health to the 'parotitis' outbreak.

verbatim: Far otherwise was it with the servant of Phanagoras, who had none of these symptoms, and died. [...] the slave of Erato and Myllus, after copious hemorrhage, lapsed into dysentery. They recovered.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While treated, slaves are identified primarily by their status as property ('servant of...', 'the slave of...') and are subjected to the same clinical recording as citizens, yet their identity is entirely dependent on the master, indicating less individual autonomy in the medical records.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to the gymnasium and physical training
verbatim: [§2] The sufferers were youths, young men, and men in their prime, usually those who frequented the wrestling school and gymnasia. Few women were attacked.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author notes that an epidemic of swellings (mumps) primarily affected those attending gymnasia and wrestling schools, then notes 'Few women were attacked.' This implies that women were not part of the group frequenting these public institutions of physical training.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§6] From all the diseases some died, but the greatest number from these fevers, especially children—those just weaned, older children of eight or ten years, and those approaching puberty.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies children as a specific biological group that suffered a higher mortality rate during the epidemic in Thasos, indicating a lack of the 'resource' of survival/protection compared to other age groups.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§6] children for the most part in such cases suffer chiefly from the convulsions; [§9] In many patients, especially children, there were convulsions and slight feverishness from the beginning.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author notes that convulsions are a specific symptom predominantly affecting the group of children during these fever outbreaks, distinguishing their clinical experience from adults.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§6] Women have both these symptoms and pains in the womb.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Gender is the criterion here; the text explicitly links the female group to a specific additional set of symptoms (pains in the womb) during the described illness.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§6] Older people, and those whose natural heat is failing, have paralysis or raving or blindness.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author distinguishes older people as a group that experiences more severe neurological and sensory resource losses (sight, mental clarity, mobility) during the fevers compared to younger cohorts.

extracted_rules.verbatim: [§6] The only good sign, the most striking that occurred, which saved very many of those who were in the greatest danger, was when there was a change to strangury... The strangury, too, came mostly to patients of the ages mentioned [children].
extracted_rules.reasoning: While children were generally more likely to die, those who developed strangury (a specific medical resource in this context) survived; the author notes this beneficial complication occurred 'mostly' in the age group previously mentioned (children).

verbatim: [§13] From all the diseases some died, but the greatest number from these fevers, especially children—those just weaned, older children of eight or ten years, and those approaching puberty.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author observes that children of specific age ranges were disproportionately affected by the mortality of the fevers described, indicating a higher societal risk and less protection from fatal outcomes for this group.

verbatim: [§13] The strangury, too, came mostly to patients of the ages mentioned [children], though it did happen to many of the others, either without their taking to bed or when they were ill.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies children as the primary group suffering from strangury (a painful urinary condition), showing that this group had less protection from such specific medical complications.

verbatim: [§15] Children for the most part in such cases suffer chiefly from the convulsions.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the medical context of the time, the author identifies children as a distinct group that lacks protection from convulsions compared to other age groups when suffering from these fevers.

verbatim: [§15] Women have both these symptoms and pains in the womb.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from sensory and motor loss
verbatim: [§15] Older people, and those whose natural heat is failing, have paralysis or raving or blindness.

verbatim: [§16] Though many women fell ill, they were fewer than the men and less frequently died.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author observes a statistical trend in the Thasos epidemic where women, as a group defined by gender, had a higher survival rate and lower infection rate compared to men.

extracted_rules.rule: Childhood protection from comatose fever mortality
verbatim: [§16] There were also comatose fevers about this time, usually in children, and of all patients these showed the lowest mortality.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies children as a specific age group that experienced a lower death rate during the outbreak of comatose fevers compared to other age groups.

verbatim: [§16] But the great majority had difficult childbirth, and after giving birth they would fall ill, and these especially died, as did the daughter of Telebulus on the sixth day after delivery.
extracted_rules.reasoning: While women generally fared better, the specific biological/health state of having given birth (post-partum) is identified as a high-risk category for mortality.

verbatim: [§16] but all to my knowledge had abortions if they chanced to fall ill when with child.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author notes a universal rule of the current constitution/epidemic where the condition of pregnancy led to the loss of the fetus (abortion) if the mother contracted the fever.

extracted_rules.rule: Youth vulnerability to ardent fever mortality
extracted_rules.group: striplings, young people, people in their prime
verbatim: [§16] And of the patients there died chiefly striplings, young people, people in their prime, the smooth, the fair-skinned, the straight-haired, the black-haired, the black-eyed, those who had lived recklessly and care-lessly, the thin-voiced, the rough-voiced, the lispers, the passionate.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly lists younger age groups as the primary victims of mortality in this specific disease environment, contrasting with children and older people mentioned elsewhere.

verbatim: [§20] Though many women fell ill, they were fewer than the men and less frequently died.

verbatim: [§21] There were also comatose fevers about this time, usually in children, and of all patients these showed the lowest mortality.

verbatim: [§23] Now the number of illnesses was great. And of the patients there died chiefly striplings, young people, people in their prime...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly links the age group of 'young people' and 'striplings' to a higher likelihood of death during the described constitution/epidemic.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to carry pregnancy to term
verbatim: [§20] ...but all to my knowledge had abortions if they chanced to fall ill when with child.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author identifies a universal negative outcome for the group of pregnant women who fall ill, effectively losing the resource of pregnancy maintenance/viability.

verbatim: [§19] But the great majority had hemorrhage, especially youths and those in the prime of life, and of these the great majority who had no hemorrhage died.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text notes that a specific physiological benefit (hemorrhage) which led to recovery was more common in younger age groups, while its absence in that same group led to death.

verbatim: [§31] [CASE IV] In Thasos the wife of Philinus gave birth to a daughter. The lochial discharge was normal, and the mother was doing well when on the fourteenth day after delivery she was seized with fever attended with rigor.
[§31] [CASE XI] The wife of Dromeades, after giving birth to a daughter, when everything had gone normally, on the second day was seized with rigor; acute fever.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text documents specific medical surveillance and systematic reporting of the health status of women following childbirth, treating the postpartum state as a distinct category for medical observation.

verbatim: [§31] [CASE IV] Pains in the genital organs. The discharge ceased. By a pessary these troubles were eased, but pains persisted in the head, neck and loins.
[§31] [CASE V] On the second day after the delivery she was seized with acute fever, pain at the stomach and in the genitals. A pessary relieved these symptoms.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text demonstrates a standard medical rule where women suffering from genital pains or suppressed discharges are treated with a specific instrument (pessary), a resource linked to their gendered physiology.

verbatim: [§31] [CASE II] Continuous throbbing of the hypochondrium; age about twenty years.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author explicitly identifies the age of the patient as a relevant clinical marker for the medical record, distinguishing young adults as a specific group within the pathological observation.

verbatim: [§31] [CASE XIII] A woman lying sick by the shore, who was three months gone with child, was seized with fever, and immediately began to feel pains in the loins.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The clinical record identifies the specific stage of pregnancy as a condition for medical observation, indicating that pregnant women were a distinct group for which specialized health data (gestation time) was tracked.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to medical treatment for postpartum complications
verbatim: [§35] In Thasos the wife of Philinus gave birth to a daughter. The lochial discharge was normal, and the mother was doing well when on the fourteenth day after delivery she was seized with fever attended with rigor. At first she suffered in the stomach and the right hypochondrium. Pains in the genital organs. The discharge ceased. By a pessary these troubles were eased, but pains persisted in the head, neck and loins.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific medical intervention (the use of a pessary) provided to a woman following childbirth to treat physical complications, indicating that postpartum women were a group recognized as requiring and receiving specific medical resources.

verbatim: [§35] A copious passing of urine with convulsions—her attendants seldom reminding her—which was white and thick, like urine with a sediment and then shaken; it stood for a long time without forming a sediment; colour and consistency like that of the urine of cattle.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The mention of 'attendants' in the context of a woman's delivery and subsequent illness indicates a social and medical norm where women in labor or suffering from female-specific illnesses are provided with a support group to assist in their care and provide reminders/monitoring.

verbatim: [§33] Continuous throbbing of the hypochondrium; age about twenty years. Eleventh day. Death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author specifically notes the age of the patient (Silenus) as a relevant factor in the clinical record, suggesting that age was a defining demographic criterion used by practitioners to categorize patients and evaluate their cases.

verbatim: [§44] CASE XIII A woman lying sick by the shore, who was three months gone with child, was seized with fever, and immediately began to feel pains in the loins.

[§45] CASE XIV Melidia, who lay sick by the temple of Hera, began to suffer violent pain in the head, neck and chest. Immediately she was attacked by acute fever, and there followed a slight menstrual flow.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text documents detailed clinical observations of women, including conditions specific to their biological group (pregnancy and menstruation), indicating they were subjects of the era's medical attention and systematic recording of health outcomes.

verbatim: [§43] CASE XII A man dined when hot and drank too much. During the night he vomited everything; acute fever; pain in the right hypochondrium; inflammation, soft underneath, from the inner part; an uncomfortable night.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author includes male subjects in his clinical case studies, documenting their symptoms and outcomes for medical knowledge, demonstrating the inclusion of men in medical observation and resource allocation.

verbatim: [§46] ...In the first place many were attacked by painful tenesmus, mostly children—all in fact who were approaching puberty—and most of these died.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author identifies a specific medical vulnerability to a fatal condition based on the age group of children approaching puberty during the described epidemic.

verbatim: [§46] Many were attacked by the erysipelas all over the body when the exciting cause was a trivial accident or a very small wound; especially when the patients were about sixty years old and the wound was in the head, however little the neglect might have been.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that advanced age (sixty years old) is a specific criterion for increased risk of severe, spreading infection from minor wounds.

verbatim: [§46] CASE VI ...The maiden daughter of Euryanax was seized with fever. ... There was also some inherited tendency to consumption.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author links the patient's condition to her lineage, identifying an 'inherited tendency' as a criterion for the disease.

verbatim: [§46] As to those with a melancholic or a rather sanguine complexion, they were attacked by ardent fevers, phrenitis and dysenteric troubles.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Hippocratic text uses physical/humoral constitution (complexion) as a near-immutable criterion to determine which specific diseases an individual is susceptible to.

verbatim: [§46] ...the chronic diarrhoea and acrid, greasy stools affected persons of a bilious temperament.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text categorizes individuals by biological temperament (bilious) to explain the occurrence of specific material health outcomes (chronic diarrhea).

verbatim: [§46] Tenesmus affected young, phlegmatic people;

Seventh day. Died, being about twenty years old.

verbatim: [§47] CASE V Chaerion, who lay sick in the house of Demaenetus, was seized with fever after drinking.
CASE VII The woman suffering from angina who lay sick in the house of Aristion...
CASE IX The woman who lodged with Tisamenus was in bed...
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text distinguishes between patients identified by public landmarks (Liars' Market, New Wall) and those cared for in private 'houses' of named individuals, indicating that certain groups have the resource of private domestic medical care based on property/social connections.

verbatim: [§47] CASE VI The maiden daughter of Euryanax was seized with fever. [...] She was silent, and did not converse at all. Depression, the patient despairing of herself.
CASE X A woman who was one of the house of Pantimides after a miscarriage was seized with fever...
CASE XI Another woman, after a miscarriage about the fifth month, the wife of Hicetas...
extracted_rules.reasoning: Unlike the male cases (Pythion, Hermocrates) who are often identified by public locations or their own names, the women are strictly identified by their relationship to a male head of house (daughter of, wife of, house of) and their clinical history is tied to domestic/reproductive events (miscarriage, delivery).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to medical observation during physiological transition
verbatim: [§76] CASE VII In Abdera the maiden who lay sick by the Sacred Way was seized with a fever of the ardent type. She was thirsty and sleepless. Menstruation occurred for the first time.
[§76] CASE XII In Larisa a maiden was seized with an acute fever of the ardent type. ... In the fever and after the crisis menstruation for the first time, for she was a young maiden.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author identifies 'maidens' (young females) and links their medical crises specifically to the timing of their first menstruation, treating this age-defined biological milestone as a critical data point in their societal/medical treatment.

verbatim: [§90] CASE XIV In Cyzicus a woman gave birth with difficult labour to twin daughters, and the lochial discharge was far from good. First day. Acute fever with shivering; painful heaviness of head and neck. Sleepless from the first, but silent, sulky and refractory.

verbatim: [§92] CASE XVI In Meliboea a youth took to his bed after being for a long time heated by drunkenness and sexual indulgence. He had shivering fits, nausea, sleeplessness, but no thirst.
